Review: The first 80% of the book was kinda meh, I really wasn't that interested in Andy's whiny thoughts. Hes just like this average stand up guy stumbling through life. Maybe it would be a better read for someone going through a breakup. The last part where we hear Jen's perspective was better and a little redeeming. It wasn't hugely revolutionary just like the rest of the book but it was at least a bit satisfying? To hear her side of the story I guess? I think this is a book for millennials still single in their 30s and to help reassure them that it's okay to be.

Title: Rest: Why You Get More Done When You Work Less
What You Are Looking For is in the Library
My Holiday in North Korea: The Funniest/Worst Place on Earth
I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings
Babel
Review:
Okay I get that this book has flaws but I loved all the characters truly. I was sooo immersed into this book i felt like I was 12 again reading Harry Potter. It was a really fun read and talks about a lot of things I think about occasionally with a lot of anger like colonialism and how other cultures are misrepresented and even exploited (still) in western countries. Thier culture, language, traditions are 'stolen' but we cannot call it so because language isn't a good that can be traded. 

There's so much more in this book I have thoughts on and like usual I have qualms about how RF Kuang ends her books. Like the third act was not my favorite part while the setup and the characters and the world building I thought were excellent. I did however really like the epilogue and the final word.

Review:
Really well written atmospheric thriller, I appreciated that it didn't do any cheap tricks and insane reveals (there were some but those just felt at par with the rest of the story) and was still a really interesting read.

I don't think I've read a book with this many povs in that many timelines before and at first I was getting a bit confused. Overall I liked that everytime I sat down to read this I was both taken right into the Adirondack landscape and also dying to know what happened next.

Review:
Hmm, little bit disappointed cause I was expecting a lot from this one. It wasn't bad, it was actually so cool to be in just that one house and I also felt that claustrophobia. I just think I don't like spicy books (there's not too much in this) and although the twist was really good, I wish it discussed one or the other aspect of the story and not both. 

There's both Isabel learning about herself and also the story of the war and how it impacts generations ahead. I would've preferred one or the other but that's just me.

Title: The Details
Review:
I actually really really enjoyed the first chapter and not so much the rest. I guess Joanna was just that girl but the others didn't interest me that much. I wanted to see if it would tell me a bit more about the author but I think each of these portraits will speak to someone else and not all of them are for everyone (there's 4 relationships/chapters in the book). 

The prose was really beautiful tho and SO many swedish places mentioned, I highlighted them and I think there has to be at least like 50.

Title: This Thing Between Us
Review:
I really enjoyed the writing of this. The way he talks about his wife was just so beautiful but simple and still very poignant. I think it was a fun, vague exploration demonic tendencies and horror aspects. 

<spoiler>Like everyone I did think the Itza aspect would be a bigger part but I'm not entirely mad about where it ended up going.</spoiler> I quite like that it kept me on my toes but I wish the concepts were just slightly more specific. I have a lot of theories ig and it's still fun to think about them but it would've been so cool to see this book explore Mexican folklore of demons and spirits and hauntings.

Either way, the experience of reading this book was so fun. I went through so many emotions, scared myself imagining it and also had my jaw drop multiple times. Super fun read!

This book covers something I've thought about a lot, the extent of damage that family vlogging does to children. I liked that it went into the longer term effects but I just wish it was more in depth. I wanted a bit more insight into the psychological impact of these kids. 

Some of the social commentary was interesting but nothing mind blowing. It was a good story, I just wanted a bit more.

Title: This Is How You Lose the Time War
CLEOPATRA AND FRANKENSTEIN
Review:
I'm kinda glad it's over. The only good thing about this book was the writing style. I actually really liked it. It's pretty and aesthetic? But both the characters were kinda unlikeable to me and it's more so that I just couldn't get myself to care about them enough.

<spoiler>Initally, I had thought that their relationship was kinda sweet and that they were cute together but everything, literally everything, significant about their relationship happens outside their pov. We skip a few months and suddenly they're getting married. We skip to a 3rd person's narrative and suddenly Frank is in love with someone else and Cleo is cheating. I think these places could have been where we felt for these characters a bit more if told from their perspective. I thought Cleo was so unbearably privileged and although Frank isn't the best judge of character - I do have to side with him on that one big fight they had at the cabin. Eleanor's chapters were quite interesting tho, she's a very interesting character. </spoiler>

Title: A Quiet Place
Review:
Really original I thought, I liked that the writing was very dry and matter of fact. Did not see the ending coming and it did feel like the last few chapters were a bit of a drag after the twist but then another twist happens and it was v satisfying actually. I feel like because it avoided the obvious catches and twists that crime stories have and treated the reader as smart I quite liked it. 

Tbf tho I'm v naive to endings and maybe others could've predicted this a mile away, I for sure did not lol. Kinda reminded me of Perfume: the story of a murderer with the writing style.

Also very interesting insights about Japanese culture and day to day stuff. Thought this was a fun read and I might have to check out another book by this author cause I've heard they're usually from the detective's perspective.

Title: Never Let Me Go
Review: Slower than my usual pace and took me a while to get through it. I think I wasn't used to this writing that's quite tangential and jumps around a lot but the last part really got me. Quite enjoyed it all in all and I think I have to read Remains of The Day now.

Title: Ghachar Ghochar
Review:
Really fun read, some parts actually had me laughing out loud. Very accu ate characters, especially thier south Indian-ness. The dynamics of the mom, Dad, the one who always stays silent, deaf/blind to everything willingly and the one that dares to ask the question. I thought it was a fun story but I'm not sure what someone that doesn't have all the contexts of a south Indian family would make of this. 

The ending was not at all what I expected. Initially my takeaway was that it was insinuating that <spoiler>Anita would just not come back just like Malati had stayed. But after a bit, the last family conversation really irked me and maybe they're saying that Chikkappa had her killed? She saw something at that window? She asked too many questions about that woman?Chikkappa is talking about having goons around that do this and also talks about all this as if it's completely normal. That would be quite dark and ig I wasn't expecting it to get that dark.</spoiler>


Another thing I thought was interesting were all the phrases/adages that I could tell were v obviously Kannada, it's almost like I could hear the Telugu counterpart. My mom even confirmed some of them hehe
